results.raw_content: Please click here for high-resolution photos Play Day to Night at the Most Spectacular Pixar Summer Fest Inviting Guests into a Bigger World of Pixar at Hong Kong Disneyland Resort HONG KONG, MAY 5, 2026 — Calling all families and Pixar Animation Studios fans to plan ahead for a Pixar-filled adventure at the first-ever Pixar Summer Fest at Hong Kong Disneyland Resort (HKDL) from June 12 through August 31, 2026. This summer, HKDL will transform into a bigger, more dynamic, and high-energy world of Pixar, where favorite Pixar pals come to life across the resort in surprising and interactive ways. From day to night, guests can play in different themed lands, featuring new entertainment high in the sky, new forms of character encounters, and a variety of playful experiences — alongside returning guest-favorite offerings. Pixar Summer Fest fills the celebration with laughter, connection, and play. Guests can jump into the fun early by securing their visit in advance, with options including Disney Premier Access, to help make the most of every moment. From themed dining experiences to hotel staycations, make sure to plan ahead for a more playful summer. For year‑round magic beyond Pixar Summer Fest, Magic Access unlocks exclusive events and privileges all year long. The all-new Pixar entertainment experience will debut next year as an immersive theatrical adventure, where guests will join beloved Pixar characters on a fun and magical journey that blends real and digital environments, celebrating the power of play and the joy of friendship. Don’t miss the last chance to enjoy the Magic Access Limited‑Time Ticket Upgrade Offer, with 10% off the regular price until June 7. All-new Pixar Pals Spectacular The all-new “Pixar Pals Spectacular,” featuring glowing drones and projections across the Castle of Magical Dreams and Main Street, U.S.A., celebrates friendship through beloved Pixar stories. It will debut as a nighttime highlight taking place before the “Momentous” nighttime spectacular. This experience will delight guests with fan-favorite Pixar characters and stories brought to life through music, storytelling, and dazzling visuals in the sky, creating unforgettable moments to share with friends. Meet and Play with Pixar Pals Across HKDL Throughout the park this summer, guests will encounter familiar faces from Pixar stories, including Toy Story, Inside Out, The Incredibles, Up, Turning Red, Coco, and Brave, each ready to jump into the seasonal fun in their own unique way. Step into the Animation Academy for a brand-new experience, where guests can draw their favorite Toy Story pals before heading next door to meet them in person! Taking magical encounters to the next level, there are lively character moments throughout the park that invite everyone to become part of the action – catch Merida from Brave, Miguel from Coco, Meilin Lee from Turning Red, and more Pixar pals for different joyful interactions throughout the day. Party with Pixar Pals on Main Street, U.S.A. The “Pixar Water Play Street Party!” returns in full scale, inviting guests to dive into a splash-filled adventure packed with refreshing fun to beat the summer heat. Along Main Street, U.S.A., guests can also sing and dance with their favorite Pixar pals during the “Friendtastic!” parade, featuring beloved characters on their signature floats in an energetic daytime celebration. Pixar-Inspired Shop and Dine Guests can enjoy a variety of Pixar-themed food and beverage offerings across the resort throughout the day, from a playful treat to a photo-worthy soft serve ice cream. At Main Street Corner Cafe Hosted by Coca-Cola®, the Pixar Dine & Play Party will turn the dining table into a playground with interactive offerings, including Make Your Own Alien Pizza and Slinky Dog Spiral Roll. A wide range of HKDL-exclusive Pixar ball-inspired collections and popular Toy Story-themed assortments will also be available, including headbands, plush toys, apparel, and stationery — perfect for adding playful touches to the summer fun. Guests can look forward to the all-new Pixar Magic Passport, the latest edition of the fan-favorite magic passport, offering more surprising ways to play while exploring the park. New interactive retail experiences further enhance the visit, making shopping a part of the adventure. At the Emporium, the Popcorn Pop-Up Shop, opening this July, invites guests to explore a variety of Pixar-themed collectibles through engaging moments with cast members. Play All the Way to the Hotels The playful spirit will continue across the resort in all shapes and forms. Guests can dive into the Finding Nemo Poolside Leisure Times* or take part in the Spirit of Adventure Craft Workshop* at Disney Explorers Lodge. The immersive fun extends to all three resort hotels, with Pixar Bento Making Workshop* and Twist & Turn Toy Story Balloon Workshop* that keep the adventure going. To elevate the ultimate summer getaway, families can transform their overnight visit into a total “play-over” with a Pixar Pals In-Room Celebration* and Pixar Pals In-Room Themed Surprises*, ensuring the fun never sleeps. Guests can book a room and breakfast buffet package for two, starting from HK$2,268, for a refreshing summer staycation that keeps the magic and playful moments going well into the night. *Booking for Pixar-themed magical hotel add-ons will start from May 12, 2026. Guests may refer to the Hong Kong Disneyland Resort official website for details when available. Plan Ahead for Vibrant Summer Now is the perfect time to plan ahead for the summer. Guests can get a head start by securing hotel stays, dining experiences, and Disney Premier Access options early to enjoy a seamless day-to-night experience. A vibrant world of Pixar will be waiting at Hong Kong Disneyland Resort, filled with familiar faces, heartwarming moments, and playful adventures unfolding all around them from day to night. To keep the magic going beyond summer, guests can become Magic Access members through the limited‑time upgrade offer, unlocking year‑round privileges including shopping and dining discounts, exclusive previews, and special events. ### *Guests are required to purchase their tickets in advance and have a valid park reservation for their visit. Please refer to the latest visit arrangements on Hong Kong Disneyland’s official website: https://www.hongkongdisneyland.com/ About Hong Kong Disneyland Resort Hong Kong Disneyland Resort offers unforgettable, culturally distinctive Disney experiences for guests of all ages and backgrounds. Filled with your favorite Disney stories and characters, Hong Kong Disneyland Resort offers guests the opportunity to explore eight diverse lands that are home to award-winning, one-of-a-kind attractions and entertainment. Complete your adventure with stays at the resort’s luxurious Disney hotels. The magic doesn’t end at our doorstep; as a dedicated member of the local community that cares deeply about societal wellbeing, Hong Kong Disneyland Resort spreads its magic through community service programs that help families in need, boost creativity among children and families, encourage the protection of the environment and inspire healthier living. Mobile app gives the latest updates & helps plan your visit The Hong Kong Disneyland mobile app keeps guests informed of all the excitement at Hong Kong Disneyland Resort, from operating hours to entertainment schedules. Reserve your park visit and get Disney Standby Pass to meet Disney friends or other designated experiences inside the park. Use the GPS-enabled map to find your way around the park or make reservations for park and hotel restaurants, and make every moment count with wait times for each attraction. Magic Access members can also view block-out calendars and membership privileges, designed to make your visit even more magical. Download the Hong Kong Disneyland mobile app for free: iOS users: https://itunes.apple.com/app/id1077550649 Android users: https://goo.gl/ZbHHbP For media inquiries, please contact: Alice Ma 5ives # Should You Book Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel Character Dining?

Character dining at Hong Kong Disneyland happens in the hotels and is worth if when traveling to Hong Kong with kids.

![Katie Dillon](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2024/07/katie-dillon-san-diego-blog-150x150.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland character dining happens at the Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/hong-kong-disneyland-character-dining.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-7.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-5.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-1-2.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-4.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-3.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-2.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-1.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland Character Dining at Disneyland Hotel Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/Hong-Kong-Disneyland-Character-Dining-at-Disneyland-Hotel-Enchanted-Garden-Restaurant-2-2.jpg.webp)
![Hong Kong Disneyland character dining happens at the Enchanted Garden Restaurant](https://lajollamom.com/wp-content/uploads/w3-webp/uploads/2013/09/hong-kong-disneyland-character-dining.jpg.webp)

Character dining at [Hong Kong Disneyland](https://lajollamom.com/hong-kong-disneyland-travel-asia/) (a must-do when in [Hong Kong with kids](https://lajollamom.com/things-to-do-in-hong-kong-with-kids/)) is actually located at the park’s two hotels which are a 5-minute shuttle ride away from the Disneyland MTR (train) station. Guests choose between Chef Mickey at Disney’s Hollywood Hotel or the Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel’s Enchanted Garden restaurant. Reservations are an absolute must as well as researching the dates and times that character dining is offered at each hotel because it isn’t always all-day or daily. Here’s what else to expect and though I tire of theme parks, I really enjoyed this experience.

## Characters at Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel’s Enchanted Garden Restaurant

I made a booking about a month in advance of our August 2013 trip to Hong Kong, only because I’ve been disappointed in the past by lack of last-minute availability (we used to live in Hong Kong). Call as soon as your trip is confirmed.

### Location in the Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel

Enchanted Garden restaurant is located down a flight of stairs from the lobby at Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel. It also happens to be right next door to [Bibbidi Bobbidi Boutique](https://lajollamom.com/hong-kong-disneyland-hotel-princess-bibbidi-bobbidi-boutique/), where girls can dress up like princesses, Minnie Mouse or Tinkerbell. Lunch and dinner are available, but if not staying at the hotel, consider booking the dress up and lunch or dinner back-to-back. If you do this, allow for about 75 minutes. The Bibbidi Bobbidi Boutique usually takes about an hour, but sometimes there’s a wait or it takes a while to receive your professional photos. A little buffer is nice.

### An International Buffet With Cocktails

The Enchanted Garden has a Victorian feel accompanied by a ton of odd faux vines and plants near the entrance. The food was much better than I anticipated with a heavy emphasis on fresh seafood from crab legs to conch. The sashimi at the Japanese station was excellent and the chef there prepares custom sushi roll orders for those willing to ask and wait a little bit. Of course, dim sum, stir fry, and other Chinese favorites compliment Indian curries, fresh salads and more. There were delicious (small) lobster tails and even a fun make-your-own soft serve ice cream station. Regardless of cuisine preference, I do think it’s possible to find something to nosh on here.

Wine and cocktails are available at an additional charge but you might need to flag a server for a refill as they aren’t constantly checking. Grab a plate of food shortly after seating as after about 20 minutes or so, the characters emerge. Do note that if you happen to be refilling plates during the time they arrive at your table, in our experience, they’ll come back. It’s sort of flawless operation in the character department.

### Appearing Characters

There is plenty of time for character interaction, actually, as they diligently visit each table (sometimes more than once). **Goofy and Pluto** were the first to come out and they each spent a few minutes with us, allowing for multiple photos. **Minnie Mouse and Mickey Mouse** each had handlers that would take group photos with guest phones and cameras so everyone at the table was in the photo.

Sure, there’s a professional photographer should you like to purchase a photo to take home at the end of the meal, but there was absolutely no pressure to do so. I appreciated how generous they were with allowing photos using guest cameras and phones—even repositioning us if the shot wasn’t right.

You may also order birthday cakes and favors in advance to jazz up a special occasion.

### Final Thoughts

The kids love it and some of the best photos from my trip are of my daughter’s ear-to-ear grins while she and her friend hugged a variety of characters. Perhaps it’s because we went for dinner between the Bibbidi Bobbidi Boutique and Disneyland causing the kids to experience Disney overload, but we had a blast.

Though we were staying in Central where prices can be higher than the rest of Hong Kong, I thought the buffet was excellent value for money. More importantly, none of us felt rushed to finish and leave the restaurant. Is it Michelin-starred cuisine? Of course not, but it’s pretty good for a Disney buffet. Actually, really good. Would I do it again? Absolutely.

Price: $488 HKD for adult and $318 HKD for kids during dinner on a weekday. Expect to pay $40-$50 more on weekends and holidays, and less for lunch. Kids under 3 years old eat free.

## Chef Mickey at Disney’s Hollywood Hotel

We haven’t done this yet but here are the details. As far as I can tell, the only character you meet is Mickey Mouse dressed up in a chef’s costume (please share if you’ve experienced differently). Kids receive a chef hat signed by Mickey, too, and an opportunity to take photos with the famous mouse. The buffet is international cuisine.

The reason why we haven’t been is that I (and my local Hong Kong friends) prefer Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel as it’s a bit nicer, though the prices reflect that on occasion. But, you can eat breakfast here daily ($148 for kids and $198 for adults) with lunch ($158 kids and $268 adults) and dinner ($268 for kids and for $438 adults) served on the weekends and holidays only. Kids under 3 years old eat for free. Do check the dates for availability and note that this is your only option for breakfast character dining at the moment.

## 8 thoughts on “Should You Book Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel Character Dining?”

I always loved going to character breakfasts and dinners with my family in Disney World, Orlando, Florida. I really want to go to Disney Hong Kong!!!

I didn’t even know this character dining thing existed until we went to WDW a few years ago. I didn’t reserve in time then but make a point to now each time I know we’re headed to a Disney park!

I called the hotel about a character breakfast and they booked me into the Enchanted garden between 7:30 and 9am. No mention of Chef Mickey’s. Is it a mistake do you think?

Hi Beth. I haven’t been to the breakfast yet. Chef Mickey’s happens at the Hollywood Hotel which is down the road from Disneyland Hotel so that’s probably why. The Enchanted Garden restaurant is at Disneyland Hotel and, from what I can tell, features more characters. Do let me know how you like it!

Hi, I am planning a trip to Disneyland for the day whilst I am in Hong Kong and would like to do character dining at Enchanted Garden. Do they have shuttles running from the parks to the hotel & once I leave the park am I able to return?

Hi! Do i need to stay at the disneyland hotel to be able to book the character dining?

Nope! Anyone can book it so you do not need to stay at the hotel. Have fun!

Thank you so much! Would you know if Explorer’s lodge character dining feature several characters as well or just mickey? And do you recommend booking character dining at enchanted garden among all the dining venues?

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Major business houses in Canton

For other uses, see [Hong (disambiguation)](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_(disambiguation) "Hong (disambiguation)").

A **hong** ([Chinese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 行; [pinyin](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *háng*; [Jyutping](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *hong4-2*) was a type of Chinese [merchant](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merchant "Merchant") establishment and its associated type of building.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Hongs arose in [Guangzhou](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") (formerly known as Canton) as intermediaries between Western and Chinese merchants during the 18th and 19th centuries under the [Canton System](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canton_System "Canton System").

## Canton (Guangzhou)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Canton (Guangzhou)")]

See also: [Cohong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cohong "Cohong") and [Thirteen Factories](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thirteen_Factories "Thirteen Factories")

The name "hong" ([Chinese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 行; [pinyin](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *háng*; [Jyutping](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *hong4*; lit. 'profession', 'row') originally referred to the row of factories built outside of the city walls of [Canton](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") near the [Pearl River](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearl_River "Pearl River").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [Thirteen Factories](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thirteen_Factories "Thirteen Factories") were used during the [Canton System](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canton_System "Canton System") period to host foreign traders and the products purchased, under the aegis of the *[cohong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cohong "Cohong")*. The hongs (or Factories) were usually owned by hong merchants such as Pan Zhencheng (also known as [Puankhequa](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puankhequa "Puankhequa")).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Cantonese hongs changed location several times after fires[[4]](#cite_note-4) and became less important after the [First Opium War](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") (1839–1842), as Canton lost its monopoly of foreign trade and Hong Kong was ceded to the British as a colony.

## Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

In Hong Kong, the name *hong* is used to designate major business houses. One of the earliest foreign hongs established there was [Jardine Matheson & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Jardine,_Matheson_&_Co. "History of Jardine, Matheson & Co."), who bought Lot No. 1 at the first Hong Kong land sale in 1841.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1843 the same firm established a mainland China headquarters on the [Bund](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Bund_(Shanghai) "The Bund (Shanghai)") in Shanghai, just south of the British Consulate. The building was known as "the Ewo Hong", or "Ewo House", based on the [Cantonese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") pronunciation of the company's Chinese name (怡和行, [Cantonese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"): *ji4 wo4 hong4*, now [怡和洋行](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jardine_Matheson "Jardine Matheson")).[[6]](#cite_note-6) Jardines took the name from the earlier *[Ewo hong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ewo_(hong) "Ewo (hong)")* run by [Howqua](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howqua "Howqua") near Whampoa, Canton.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The term is most often used in reference to [colonial Hong Kong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") companies.

Prior to the establishment of banking institutions other than small foreign bank branches, the three firms that financed most of Hong Kong's economic activities were [Jardine Matheson & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jardine_Matheson_&_Co. "Jardine Matheson & Co."), [Dent & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dent_&_Co. "Dent & Co."), and [Russell & Company](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russell_&_Company "Russell & Company").[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8) Most of these firms became multinational corporations with management consisting of largely European expatriates.[[9]](#cite_note-Genzberger-9)

By the time of the handover of Hong Kong to China in 1997, many of the hongs had diversified their holdings and shifted their headquarters elsewhere to avoid potential takeover by the [Chinese Communist Party](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communist_Party_of_China "Communist Party of China").[[9]](#cite_note-Genzberger-9)

## Conglomerates of colonial Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Conglomerates of colonial Hong Kong")]

Note: *Below are lists of companies that had a predominant effect on Hong Kong's economy at a particular era. Their noteworthiness is debatable. The official names of the era are used.*

### 1843

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1843")]

* 12 large British firms[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8)
* Six Indian [Parsee](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsee "Parsee") companies – including D.M. Rustomjee
* One American company – [Augustine Heard and Company](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augustine_Heard_and_Company "Augustine Heard and Company")

### 1844

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1844")]

* [Jardine Matheson](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Jardine,_Matheson_&_Co. "History of Jardine, Matheson & Co.")[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8)
* [Dent & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dent_&_Co. "Dent & Co.")

### 1850s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1850s")]

* [Russell & Co](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russell_&_Company "Russell & Company") – U.S. company founded by [Samuel Russell](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Russell "Samuel Russell") in Canton in 1824 for the opium trade; acquired J & T H Perkins of Boston in 1830, established Hong Kong office in 1850
* [Wheelock Marden](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wheelock_&_Co "Wheelock & Co") 1857

### 1860s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1860s")]

* Gilman and Bowman – established by Richard James Gilman as a tea trader in 1840; taken over by Duncan Paterson of Australia 1917 and turned into a privately held company; bought by [Inchcape Group](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchcape_plc "Inchcape plc") in 1969[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### 1870s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1870s")]

* [Butterfield and Swire](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swire_Group "Swire Group")[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8)
* [Adamson Bell and Company](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dodwell_&_Co. "Dodwell & Co.") - transformed into [Dodwell, Carlill & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dodwell_&_Co. "Dodwell & Co.") in 1891 by George Benjamin Dodwell; changed name to [Dodwell & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dodwell_&_Co. "Dodwell & Co.") in 1899; bought by [Inchcape Group](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchcape_plc "Inchcape plc") in 1972
* Hutchison International - founded in 1877 by John Hutchison, now part of [Hutchison Whampoa](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hutchison_Whampoa "Hutchison Whampoa")

### 1880s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1880s")]

* [The Wharf (Holdings)](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wharf_(Holdings) "The Wharf (Holdings)")

### 1890s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1890s")]

* [Jebsen & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jebsen_Group "Jebsen Group")

•

Han Hong is a Chinese singer, songwriter and philanthropist of mixed Tibetan and Han ethnicity. I do not mean to be difficult, but it depends on which dialect you are using, which tone it refers to and whether there is a second character.

Dialect. The most famous Hong that the world knows is the Hong in Hong Kong (香港). In this context, Hong is a Cantonese word meaning fragrant (in Jyutping it would now be written hoeng1, which is much closer to the way it is pronounced in Cantonese). Thus Hong Kong means fragrant harbour, a name it got because of the trade in agarwood (Aquilaria sinensis). However, I suspect this is not what you are referring to unless you are eluding to 1997 and the events thereafter.

PS - Hong in English refers to warehouse and was influenced by Cantonese and the fact that there were a lot of waterfront warehouses which the English incorrect though were called Hong.

Tone. If you are referring to Hong in Mandarin, then it matters which tone you are using. Mandarin has 4 tones or inflections and a neutral tone. Meaning the Hong can refer to one of 5 characters. One of these characters is 鸿. Without a second character it is not possible to be certain what it means as it can be an adjective or noun despite the third radical,鸟, meaning bird. That this for example:

两 位 领 导 人 之 间 的 鸿 沟 无 法 弥 合

The sentence reads “the gulf between the two statesmen cannot be bridge”. Here 鸿 is paired with 沟 and means gulf or chasm. On the other hand, 惊 鸿 means graceful.

I suspect the word you are looking for is the hong in 红色 which is pronounced Hóngsè in Mandarin and means red.

Second Character. Even if I got your first character right, the second character can make a lot of difference. While less frequent, this occurs in English as well. For example, the word ‘awe’ is quite different when matched with ‘full’ as in ‘awful’ (though when first coined it did mean full of awe). For example:

红酒 means wine and can refer to any type of western grape wine (as opposed Chinese rice wine)

红薯 means sweet potato, probably because Chinese sweet potatoes had a redness purple skin

I mportance of the colour Red. But as I mentioned above, I suspect the Hong you are looking for is 红色 Hóngsè or the colour red.

You would be right to say that red is everywhere in China today. It is the red envelops given at Chinese New Year, in the red cloth that adorns doors during auspicious occasions, it is widely referred to in poems, songs and literature and is the dominant colour in China’s flag. I could go on for a long time as to why this is but here is a summary:

*   The first reason was that it was easily available. Red hematite rock was readily available and used. The processed powder from such rocks were found in a tomb near Beijing dating back 700,000 years.
*   The earliest use of red in a man made object in China dates back to pottery fragments which came from 5000 to 3000 BC. The pots were coloured black and red, very striking. This suggests that red may have been associated with substance and thus life force in general.
*   In the Han dynasty (202 BC – 9 AD, 25–220 AD), artisans used red dye from madder plants for cloths and for lacquerware.
*   The term red started to appear in literature. The most famous was “Writing a Poem on a Crimson Leaf”. Written during the Tang dynasty (618–907 AD) the story is about a courtesan who writes a love poem on a red leaf which she then casts on the waterway in front of her window. The man finds it and floats a replying poem back. The courtesan falls in love with the unknown writer (its more sentimental that my bland summary). When the courtesan is finally allowed to marry, she discovers that she is married to the man who replied. They lived happy ever after (which was rare for courtesans). Thus red cemented as auspicious and happy.
*   This combination of availability and substance, life, auspiciousness and love reinforced itself. By the time it came to the Qing Dynasty (the last dynasty in China but not a Chinese dynasty) the Qing’s also linked it with fire.
*   Mao liked this association as it meant burning the old and making way for the new or progress. During the height of the Cultural Revolution, the traffic signals were changed so that red was ‘go’ instead of ‘green’.

In summary, the colour red has extremely strong connotations in Chinese culture. The reason appear to be availability and its use in objects that suggested sustenance and live. As more source of red were found, its use was extended and, I suspect, to sell red objects for a higher price, the intangible value was hipped up. Having said that, independent of commerce, Chinese literature started using red as a symbol for good fortune and love. The availability, profitability and literary symbolism reinforced itself. Then after 1949, politics was added until today red means many things and comes with a heavy historical baggage, abet a happy baggage.

Haha, there’s something you might not believe: despite China being the largest atheist country in the world — and probably the one that values technology and believes in science the most — many Chinese people believe in the power of mantras.

That includes me, someone who cares deeply about mathematics, physics, and engineering.

The most popular mantra is the 准提咒 Zhunti Mantra (Cundi Mantra).

This mantra has no restrictions. You can eat meat, drink alcohol, and even have “illicit sexual relations” (in Buddhist terms, “licit sexual relations” refers only to “normal” intercourse between legally married couple; everything else is “illicit” — different from the legal definition).

You also don’t have to be a Buddhist at all.

As long as you recite this mantra a certain number of times, your wish will come true.

The wish can be purely secular — such as earning more money, recovering from illness, or having a smooth career — with no religious meaning whatsoever.

However, the wish cannot harm others.

For example, wishing someone to fall seriously ill is not allowed.

The wish also shouldn’t be too big, like wishing for world peace — that’s too difficult.

(EDIT:I was wrong. You can pray for world peace, and it will even be praised and encouraged. It’s just that most people who practice this mantra do it to solve their own worldly problems, such as wanting to make more money.）

This mantra is very short; you can recite it in just a moment.

![Image 21](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-035501e4492bb88ac6817782a65eaae8)

The picture shows the phonetic transcription.

As for how many repetitions are needed for results, it’s hard to say.

Some say they wished for their body odor to disappear and it took only 5,000 recitations.

Others say they wanted their child to turn over a new leaf, so the couple together recited it 800,000 times.

If you want to give it a try but don’t know how to pronounce it, I found a pronunciation version on YouTube — you can follow along a few times, and it’s so short you’ll have it memorized in no time.

If you don’t like this tone or rhythm, that’s fine — you can search for “准提咒” in Chinese, and you’ll find plenty of different recitations. Just pick one you like.

There are many counting methods. Traditionally, people used prayer beads. Nowadays, most people just buy a finger counter from Taobao: for example, press once every 4 recitations; when the counter reaches 100, that means you’ve recited it 400 times.

![Image 22](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-16d618eeaa674f1bfc4e2c8f3b5d7e43)

Why do I recite it? Because it works.

As Comrade Deng Xiaoping famously said: “It doesn’t matter whether a cat is black or white; as long as it catches mice, it’s a good cat.”

The premise of his statement was that at the time, China was in the midst of a heated debate over whether certain practices counted as “capitalist.”

For example, according to Marx’s writings, hiring more than seven workers would already classify someone as a capitalist — yet some private enterprises at the time were employing over 200 people.

It was in this context that Deng Xiaoping made his famous assertion: “Let’s not argue for now about whether it is capitalism; let’s see whether it can raise production levels.”

It’s a bit like this mantra: don’t argue over whether it’s superstition — just see if it works.

At the time, many “capitalists” were deeply worried about the possibility of being politically targeted or having their assets confiscated in the future. Some officials came up with a way to ease their fears: they asked the capitalists to write a letter stating they were willing to donate their factories to the state free of charge. The officials would then write an official note on it: “Very good, very patriotic — we will not accept it for now.”

With this signed note in hand, the “capitalists” felt much more at ease. Even if there was political persecution in the future, they could show the document and say: “Look, I’ve been patriotic all along — I was ready to donate my factory to our great motherland long ago!”

Looking back at the 1980s now, it truly feels like a different world.

~~

It’s the same with the Zhunti Mantra — if it solves my real-life problems, that’s what matters most.

As for the underlying principle… I’m curious about that too.

~~~

My personal guess goes like this: Suppose there is a deserted island (Earth), and countless people have unfortunately been stranded there, all having suffered head injuries that caused amnesia.

At this point, a rescuer (the Buddha) voluntarily comes to the island and tells you, “Keep writing SOS in the sand.” You’re skeptical, but one day, an iron bird (a helicopter) actually comes to rescue you!

Advanced liberation-type mantras are like SOS—they allow you to directly leave this miserable world.

But many people don’t believe it, so in order to make you believe, the rescuer hides up in a tree and secretly picks a coconut, then tells you, “If you sincerely recite a certain mantra (the Cundi Mantra), you’ll have food to eat.”

You try reciting it in reverse disbelief, and the rescuer drops a coconut.

To your surprise, it really works!

At that point, you might start having faith in SOS as well.

That’s my explanation.

My wife, who is Chinese, and I have discussed this topic, but not to a great extent.

In my view (as an outsider looking in), Chinese culture has endured several upheavals:

1.   Chinese culture is very rich and centuries (if not millennia) old
2.   Having a very rich and very old culture builds invisible restrictions in activity, flexibility, and outward initiative - the very things you celebrate are the very things that keep you from progressing.
3.   The Cultural Revolution, for all its excesses and faults (and there were many), sought to shake up the established order and allow the creation of a new paradigm for China, but it could never be permanently enforced nor should it have been - it was brutal but effective as a temporary movement, it would have been disastrous as a permanent one. More like a “cultural tsunami” if you will.
4.   The Post-Cultural Revolution allowed the parts of the new paradigm to form a newer foundation (in the economy, education, science, technology and other vital sectors)
5.   The Post-Post Cultural Restoration was to allow the resplendent and vibrant (and ancient) culture to be celebrated on top of the newer established paradigm

I'll try to clear it up a bit… as a practising Chinese!

Chinese people are sticklers of centuries old traditions and festivals and festivities..Festivals, such as the Chinese New Year aka Lunar New Year, and Spring Festival.

They are a reflection of the country's traditions and values.

Chinese appreciate art and it includes painting, sculpture, architecture, pottery, jade carving, and numerous other fine art forms.

Chinese culture places a strong emphasis on respecting and honouring elders. It is common practice to address older individuals with proper titles.

Chinese elevate calligraphy to an art form. The ink brush, ink, paper, and inkstone are the essential elements of Chinese calligraphy; and it has so many different characteristics and grade.

![Image 26](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-2b46ffe56107f2b6553252e4db4b7ced)

Chinese love to drink tea, which is more than just a drink. It's a conduit for cultural exchange. A Gong Fu Cha (工夫茶) ceremony is a way to observe the artistry and respect for the tradition of tea drinking in China.

Chinese love to eat!

In Chinese culture, food is placed in the middle of the table and everyone shares. A typical meal starts with appetizers, cold dishes, followed by several dishes - various meat, plant-based food and fresh cooked vegetables. That's usually for dinner.

Chinese people adhere to traditional festivals for many reasons, including their religious and spiritual beliefs, and the meaning behind the festivals.

Some traditional Chinese festivals include:

![Image 27](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-7737ac01603424bce97387cbfc31845e)

*   Dragon Boat Festival (Duan Wu Jie is also known as Duan Yang, which means “Upright Sun” or “Double Fifth”)

It is celebrated on the 5th day of the 5th lunar month. This festival commemorates the death of an ancient famous Chinese scholar, 屈原 Qu Yuan. Duan Wu Jie is celebrated more in the south than the northern region.
*   Mid-Autumn Festival

Celebrated on the 15th day of the 8th lunar month, this festival features moon cakes, which symbolize family reunion.
*   Chinese New Year

Also known as the Spring Festival, this is the most important festival in China. It's celebrated for 15 days and is a time for family reunions, feasting, and gift-giving. This time round it falls on January 25–26th 2025.
*   Qingming Festival

This important annual festival involves visiting ancestral tombs to sweep them and make offerings.
*   Qixi Festival

Also known as the Qiqiao Festival or Seven Sister's Birthday, this festival is celebrated on the 7th day of the 7th lunar month. It's based on the legend of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl.

Other traditional Chinese festival customs include:

![Image 28](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-854eb9c4973454046b0b24d68d0d887d)

*   Pasting scrolls, the character "Fu", meaning “luck”, and paper-cut red coloured pictures
*   Setting off firecrackers and fireworks, and lion dance performance
*   Paying New Year visits to family relatives and friends

![Image 29](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-6acb1fd2b16a56b28288a141c56afeb1)

*   Eating jiaozi (dumplings). If I were to pick a food that says “Chinese” it'd be this!
*   Watching lanterns and fireworks
*   Performing folk dances
*   Eating yuanxiao or tang yuan glutinous rice balls with a sweet filling. This is the iconic food during the Lantern Festival (15th day of the Spring Festival); as well as the Winter Solstice Festival (dongzhi) this year it was on 21 December.

![Image 30](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-d0bbdfbe54d88cc1dfc6e88bc9f120e0)

*   I just had my tang yuan a few days ago, in Kuala Lumpur.
